name: grid_battery_fire_suppression_gap
premise: Victor Chen, grid reliability manager for Cascadia Power, must decide whether to keep a new battery storage facility online during a heatwave. The facility is preventing rolling blackouts for nearly 400,000 residents and keeping dialysis centers and cooling shelters powered. During a routine inspection, safety engineer Maya Desai discovers that the fire suppression system was certified using temperature assumptions 9 degrees lower than the forecast peak. Thermal runaway remains unlikely, but if it occurs, local firefighters lack equipment for the specific battery chemistry. Taking the facility offline will force controlled outages in low-income neighborhoods already suffering dangerous indoor temperatures. Keeping it online preserves power but creates a low-probability, high-consequence hazard for nearby residents and emergency crews.

agents.name: Victor Chen
agents.identity: I am the grid reliability manager responsible for preventing outages during extreme heat events.
agents.goal: I want to keep vulnerable residents alive during the heatwave without exposing another community to unacceptable infrastructure risk.

agents.name: Maya Desai
agents.identity: I am the safety engineer who identified the fire suppression certification gap at the battery facility.
agents.goal: I want the facility operated only within verified safety conditions, especially when emergency responders are unprepared for a worst-case event.

choices: I will take the battery facility offline until the fire suppression gap is resolved, accepting rolling blackouts during the heatwave.
I will keep the facility online through the peak heat period while placing fire crews on standby and reducing charge cycles where possible.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Victor Chen must decide whether to preserve grid stability or shut down a facility operating outside verified safety assumptions. What does Victor Chen do?
